subject: maths
title: Polynomials

nodes.title: Relate zeroes and coefficients of a polynomial
nodes.brief: The chapter's top skill: given a quadratic or cubic polynomial, find its zeroes and connect them to the coefficients via the sum/product relations (and the reverse — build a polynomial from its zeroes).
nodes.quote: are the zeroes of a quadratic polynomial also related to its coefficients?
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Find zeroes and read off coefficient relations.
A zero of p(x) is a value k where p(k) = 0 (an x-intercept on the graph). For a quadratic ax²+bx+c with zeroes alpha and beta: sum alpha+beta = -b/a and product alpha*beta = c/a. These two formulas give the link between every zero and the coefficients.

PART 2 — Verify zeroes against the coefficient formulas.
Given the zeroes, compute their sum and product and check they equal -b/a and c/a. Example: 2x²-5x+3 has zeroes 1 and 3/2; sum = 1+3/2 = 5/2 = -(-5)/2 ✓ and product = 1*3/2 = 3/2 = 3/2 ✓.

PART 3 — Reverse: build a polynomial from sum and product.
Given sum S and product P, the monic quadratic is x²-Sx+P. For S=5, P=6: x²-5x+6 = (x-2)(x-3), zeroes 2 and 3, confirming sum=5 and product=6 ✓.
keyMoves: State sum and product of zeroes in terms of coefficients
Verify or construct a polynomial from its zeroes
misconceptions: Thinking zeroes equal the coefficients themselves rather than being related by −b/a and c/a

nodes.title: Identify the degree of a polynomial
nodes.brief: Read off the highest power of the variable as the degree of the polynomial.
nodes.quote: the highest power of x in p(x) is called the degree of the polynomial p(x)
nodes.explanation: Degree is the highest exponent of the variable. 4x+2 has degree 1; 2y²−3y+4 has degree 2; the degree-6 example 7u⁶−... has degree 6. This is the bedrock skill for classifying linear/quadratic/cubic.
keyMoves: Pick the highest exponent of the variable
misconceptions: Confusing the leading coefficient with the degree
Counting the number of terms instead of the highest power

nodes.title: Classify a polynomial as linear, quadratic, or cubic
nodes.brief: Name a polynomial by its degree: degree 1 linear, degree 2 quadratic, degree 3 cubic.
nodes.quote: A polynomial of degree 1 is called a linear polynomial.
nodes.explanation: Once the degree is known, the polynomial gets a name: degree 1 = linear, degree 2 = quadratic (general form ax²+bx+c, a≠0), degree 3 = cubic (general form ax³+bx²+cx+d, a≠0).
misconceptions: Calling 2x+5−x² linear because it starts with a linear term — it is actually quadratic

nodes.title: Recognise expressions that are not polynomials
nodes.brief: Reject expressions with the variable in a denominator or under a root as non-polynomials.
nodes.quote: Expressions like 1/(x − 1), √x + 2, 1/(x² + 2x + 3) etc., are not polynomials.
nodes.explanation: A polynomial requires non-negative integer powers of the variable. Expressions with the variable in a denominator (1/(x−1)) or under a radical (√x+2) are not polynomials.
keyMoves: Check each term for a negative or fractional power of the variable
Reject if the variable appears in a denominator or under a root
misconceptions: Accepting √x+2 as a polynomial because it looks simple

nodes.title: Evaluate a polynomial p(x) at x = k
nodes.brief: Substitute a number for the variable and compute the resulting value p(k).
nodes.quote: putting x = 2 in the polynomial, we get p(2) = 2² − 3 × 2 − 4 = − 6
nodes.explanation: p(k) is found by replacing every x with k and evaluating. For p(x)=x²−3x−4, p(2)=2²−3×2−4=−6. This is the substitute-and-compute bedrock used to test zeroes.
keyMoves: Replace x by k in every term
Apply order of operations to compute the number
misconceptions: Mishandling signs when k is negative, e.g. (−1)² becoming −1

nodes.title: Evaluate a polynomial at a negative argument
nodes.brief: Substitute a negative number, correctly squaring/cubing the sign.
nodes.explanation: When k is negative, (−1)² = 1 and the middle term −3×(−1) = +3. Careful sign handling gives p(−1)=0, showing −1 is a zero. This generic signed-substitution skill is reused across the chapter (e.g. verifying cubic zeroes).
keyMoves: Track the sign of each term before summing
misconceptions: Writing (−1)² = −1
Dropping the double negative in −3×(−1)

nodes.title: Define a zero of a polynomial
nodes.brief: A real number k is a zero of p(x) precisely when p(k)=0.
nodes.quote: a real number k is said to be a zero of a polynomial p(x), if p(k) = 0.
nodes.explanation: The defining property of a zero: substituting it makes the polynomial evaluate to 0. This is the criterion every zero-finding and zero-verifying step relies on.
misconceptions: Thinking a zero is where the polynomial equals the constant term rather than 0

nodes.title: Find the zero of a linear polynomial
nodes.brief: Set ax+b=0 and solve to get the single zero −b/a.
nodes.quote: the zero of the linear polynomial ax + b is −b/a = −(Constant term)/(Coefficient of x).
nodes.explanation: A linear polynomial ax+b has exactly one zero, found by solving ax+b=0 ⇒ k=−b/a. Example: zero of 2x+3 is −3/2. This is the first coefficient-to-zero relationship.
keyMoves: Solve the linear equation for x
misconceptions: Forgetting the negative sign: writing b/a instead of −b/a

nodes.title: Interpret a zero as an x-intercept of the graph
nodes.brief: Read the zeroes of p(x) as the x-coordinates where y=p(x) crosses the x-axis.
nodes.quote: the zeroes of a quadratic polynomial ax² + bx + c, a ≠ 0, are precisely the x-coordinates of the points where the parabola representing y = ax² + bx + c intersects the x-axis.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The zero–intercept connection.
Every zero of p(x) is the x-coordinate of a point where the graph y=p(x) meets the x-axis. At those points y=p(x)=0, which is exactly the definition of a zero — graph language and algebra are describing the same thing.

PART 2 — Apply the rule to a linear graph.
For y=2x+3 the line meets the x-axis when 2x+3=0, i.e. at x=−3/2, giving the single crossing point (−3/2, 0). So the only zero of 2x+3 is −3/2 — read directly from the x-axis crossing.

PART 3 — Apply to a parabola and count correctly.
For y=x²−3x−4 the parabola meets the x-axis at (−1, 0) and (4, 0), so the two zeroes are −1 and 4. Common traps: the y-intercept (where the curve crosses the y-axis) is not a zero; the vertex x-coordinate is not a zero unless the vertex sits on the x-axis.
keyMoves: Locate where the curve meets the x-axis
Read each x-coordinate as a zero
misconceptions: Reading the y-intercept instead of the x-intercept
Using the vertex x-value as a zero

nodes.title: Count the number of zeroes from a graph
nodes.brief: Count how many times the graph of y=p(x) meets the x-axis to get the number of zeroes.
nodes.quote: The number of zeroes is 2 as the graph intersects the x-axis at two points.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Count x-axis crossings, not other features.
The number of zeroes of y = p(x) equals the number of times the graph meets the x-axis. One x-intercept → 1 zero, two x-intercepts → 2 zeroes. Do not count turning points, the y-intercept, or any feature that is not an x-axis meeting point.

PART 2 — Handle tangential touches carefully.
When the graph only touches (is tangent to) the x-axis without crossing, that point counts as one zero — not two. A tangential touch is still an x-intercept; it just has multiplicity 2 algebraically, but there is still only one distinct zero at that x-value.
keyMoves: Count intersection points with the x-axis
Report that count as the number of zeroes
misconceptions: Counting turning points instead of x-axis crossings
Counting tangential touches as two zeroes

nodes.title: Bound the number of zeroes by the degree
nodes.brief: A polynomial of degree n has at most n zeroes.
nodes.quote: a polynomial p(x) of degree n has at most n zeroes.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — State the bounding rule: degree n gives at most n zeroes.
A polynomial of degree n has at most n real zeroes. A quadratic (degree 2) can have 0, 1, or 2 real zeroes; a cubic (degree 3) can have 1, 2, or 3. The degree is an upper bound, not the exact count.

PART 2 — Apply the rule to specific polynomials.
For a polynomial of degree n, count zeroes by counting x-axis crossings on its graph — there can be at most n. Example: x²+1 has 0 real zeroes (graph never touches x-axis); (x−1)² has 1; x²−5x+6 has 2. Never assume there are exactly n.
keyMoves: State the maximum number of zeroes as n
misconceptions: Assuming a degree-n polynomial always has exactly n real zeroes

nodes.title: Factorise a quadratic by splitting the middle term
nodes.brief: Split the middle term into two terms whose product equals (coeff of x²)×(constant)×x², then group.
nodes.explanation: To factorise ax²+bx+c, split bx into two terms whose product is the a×c term (here 6×2x²=12x²), then factor by grouping. 2x²−8x+6 splits as −6x−2x and factors to 2(x−1)(x−3).
keyMoves: Find two numbers with that product summing to b
misconceptions: Choosing split terms that sum to b but whose product is not a×c
Sign errors in the grouped factors

nodes.title: Factorise using the difference-of-squares identity
nodes.brief: Apply a²−b²=(a−b)(a+b) to factor a quadratic with no middle term.
nodes.explanation: Example 3 uses a²−b²=(a−b)(a+b): x²−3 = x²−(√3)² = (x−√3)(x+√3), giving zeroes √3 and −√3. This is the identity-based factoring route for ax²+c type quadratics.
misconceptions: Trying to factor x²−3 over integers and concluding it cannot be factored

nodes.title: Find zeroes from factored form via zero-product
nodes.brief: Set each linear factor equal to zero and solve to get the zeroes.
nodes.quote: the value of p(x) = 2x² − 8x + 6 is zero when x − 1 = 0 or x − 3 = 0, i.e., when x = 1 or x = 3.
nodes.explanation: Once factored as (x−1)(x−3) (times a constant), the polynomial is zero exactly when a factor is zero. Each factor gives one zero. This is the zero-product step turning factors into zeroes.
misconceptions: Reading the zero as the constant inside the factor with the wrong sign (e.g. x−3 giving −3)

nodes.title: Verify the sum of zeroes equals −b/a
nodes.brief: Check that the sum of the two zeroes equals −(coefficient of x)/(coefficient of x²).
nodes.quote: Sum of its zeroes = 1 + 3 = 4 = −(−8)/2 = −(Coefficient of x)/(Coefficient of x²)
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — State the sum formula and memorise the minus.
For a quadratic ax²+bx+c with zeroes α and β, the sum is α+β = −b/a. The minus on b is the most common error: writing b/a instead of −b/a gives the wrong sign. Remember: sum gets the MINUS; product does not.

PART 2 — Compute the sum and −b/a from the coefficients.
Add the two zeroes. For 2x²−8x+6 with zeroes 1 and 3: α+β = 1+3 = 4. Also −b/a = −(−8)/2 = 8/2 = 4. They match ✓.

PART 3 — Verify and flag the two most common errors.
If α+β = −b/a the sum relation is verified. Common errors: (1) using b/a without the minus → gets −4 instead of 4; (2) dividing by the constant c instead of by the leading coefficient a.
misconceptions: Forgetting the leading negative sign in −b/a
Dividing by the constant instead of by a

nodes.title: Verify the product of zeroes equals c/a
nodes.brief: Check that the product of the two zeroes equals (constant term)/(coefficient of x²).
nodes.quote: Product of its zeroes = 1 × 3 = 3 = 6/2 = (Constant term)/(Coefficient of x²)
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Identify the product of zeroes formula.
For a quadratic ax²+bx+c with zeroes α and β, the product is αβ = c/a. Note: no minus sign — only the sum relation carries the negative (α+β = −b/a). Confusing the two is the most common sign error on this topic.

PART 2 — Compute the product and the ratio c/a.
Multiply the two zeroes and compute c/a from the coefficients. For 2x²−8x+6 with zeroes 1 and 3: αβ = 1×3 = 3; c/a = 6/2 = 3. They match ✓.

PART 3 — State the verification.
If αβ = c/a the product relation is verified. If they do not match, recheck the zeroes or the coefficient read-off — do not add a minus sign to 'fix' the mismatch.
misconceptions: Putting a negative sign on c/a (only the sum carries the negative)

nodes.title: State the general sum/product relations for a quadratic
nodes.brief: Recall α+β=−b/a and αβ=c/a for zeroes of ax²+bx+c.
nodes.quote: sum of zeroes = α + β = −b/a = −(Coefficient of x)/(Coefficient of x²),
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — State both formulas for the general quadratic ax²+bx+c.
If α, β are the two zeroes of ax²+bx+c (a≠0), then sum α+β = −b/a and product αβ = c/a. The negative sign belongs to the sum formula, not the product formula.

PART 2 — Identify a, b, c correctly from the polynomial.
Write the polynomial in standard form before reading off a, b, c. For x²−5x+6: a=1, b=−5, c=6; so α+β = −(−5)/1 = 5 and αβ = 6/1 = 6. A missing term means the coefficient is 0.
misconceptions: Swapping which relation gets the negative sign

nodes.title: Build a quadratic from given sum and product of zeroes
nodes.brief: Use α+β=−b/a, αβ=c/a with a=1 to write a quadratic with the given sum and product.
nodes.quote: So, one quadratic polynomial which fits the given conditions is x² + 3x + 2.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The monic form: x²−Sx+P.
If the zeroes have sum S and product P, the simplest (a=1) quadratic is x²−Sx+P. The coefficient of x is −S (negative of the sum) and the constant term is P.

PART 2 — Apply: sum=−3, product=2.
b = −(sum) = −(−3) = 3 and c = product = 2, giving x²+3x+2. Check: zeroes are −1 and −2; sum = −1+(−2) = −3 ✓ and product = (−1)(−2) = 2 ✓. Any non-zero scalar multiple k·(x²+3x+2) also has the same zeroes.
misconceptions: Using b=sum instead of b=−sum
Forgetting the polynomial form is x²−(sum)x+(product)

nodes.title: Verify a value is a zero of a cubic by substitution
nodes.brief: Substitute a candidate into the cubic and confirm the result is 0.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Substitute the candidate value into every term.
Replace every x with the candidate. For p(x) = ax³+bx²+cx+d, write a(candidate)³ + b(candidate)² + c(candidate) + d without evaluating yet — write out the full substitution first so no term is accidentally dropped.

PART 2 — Evaluate each term carefully, then sum.
Compute each term in order. For a negative candidate, keep track of the sign: (−k)³ = −k³, (−k)² = +k². For a fractional candidate keep everything as fractions throughout. Sum the four results.

PART 3 — Confirm the total is 0.
If the sum equals 0 the candidate is a zero. If not, the candidate is not a zero — recheck each term for a slip rather than concluding the candidate is correct.
keyMoves: Substitute the candidate into the cubic
misconceptions: Arithmetic slips computing higher powers
Mishandling fractional or negative arguments

refreshers.trigger: raising a fraction to a power and adding fractions over a common denominator
refreshers.surfacingQuestion: quick one before the fractional candidate: what is (2/3)^2, and how do you add 1/2 + 1/3?
ladder: A fraction a/b means a divided by b, with a on top and b on the bottom.
Raising a fraction to a power raises the top and the bottom separately: (a/b)^2 = a^2/b^2, and (a/b)^3 = a^3/b^3.
To add two fractions you first rewrite them over the same (common) denominator, then add the tops and keep that denominator.
Whole numbers count too - a number like 4 is just 4/1, so it can be put over a common denominator with the others.
refreshers.answer: For a fraction, a power hits the top and the bottom on their own: (2/3)^2 = 2^2/3^2 = 4/9, and (a/b)^3 = a^3/b^3. To add fractions you put them over one common denominator, add the numerators, and keep that denominator: 1/2 + 1/3 = 3/6 + 2/6 = 5/6. A plain whole number is just that number over 1, so it joins in over the common denominator too. In this node, when the candidate is a fraction you square it and cube it this way, write every one of the four terms over a common denominator, add the numerators, and check the total comes out to 0/something = 0.
refreshers.returnCue: with powers of a fraction and adding over a common denominator handy, you can carry the fractional candidate all the way through and check the sum hits 0.

nodes.title: State the sum/pair-sum/product relations for a cubic
nodes.brief: Recall α+β+γ=−b/a, αβ+βγ+γα=c/a, αβγ=−d/a for ax³+bx²+cx+d.
nodes.quote:
α + β + γ = −b/a,
αβ + βγ + γα = c/a,
α β γ = −d/a.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Identify the four coefficients.
For ax³ + bx² + cx + d, read off a (the leading coefficient), b (the x² coefficient), c (the x coefficient), and d (the constant term). Be careful with embedded signs: in 2x³ − 5x² + 7x − 3 the values are a=2, b=−5, c=7, d=−3 — do not strip the minus signs.

PART 2 — Write the three symmetric relations and remember the signs.
With zeroes α, β, γ: sum α+β+γ = −b/a (minus); pair-sum αβ+βγ+γα = c/a (no minus); product αβγ = −d/a (minus again). Memory aid: minus on sum and product, no minus on pair-sum.
misconceptions: Dropping the negative on αβγ=−d/a
Putting a negative on the pair-sum c/a

nodes.title: Build a quadratic from transformed zeroes (α², β² etc.)
nodes.brief: When zeroes transform (e.g. to α², β²), compute the new sum and product via α²+β²=(α+β)²−2αβ and α²β²=(αβ)², then write the polynomial.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Compute the new sum using the algebraic identity.
If α and β are zeroes of the original polynomial, find α+β = −b/a and αβ = c/a first. Then use the identity α²+β² = (α+β)² − 2αβ to get the sum of the new zeroes. This avoids finding α and β individually.

PART 2 — Compute the new product using (αβ)².
The product of the new zeroes α² and β² equals (αβ)². So α²β² = (c/a)². Both the new sum and new product are now expressed entirely in terms of the original coefficients.

PART 3 — Write the new quadratic as x²−(new sum)x+(new product).
With new sum S = α²+β² and new product P = α²β², the monic quadratic is x²−Sx+P. Clear fractions by multiplying through by a suitable integer if needed. Example: 6x²−5x+1 gives α+β=5/6, αβ=1/6; S=(5/6)²−2(1/6)=25/36−12/36=13/36; P=(1/6)²=1/36; polynomial x²−(13/36)x+1/36, i.e. 36x²−13x+1.
keyMoves: Extract α+β = −b/a and αβ = c/a from the original polynomial
Compute new sum α²+β² = (α+β)²−2αβ and new product α²β² = (αβ)²
Build the new quadratic x²−(new sum)x+(new product), clearing fractions if needed
misconceptions: Trying to find α and β individually instead of using symmetric formulas
Using α²+β² = (α+β)² (forgetting the −2αβ correction term)
Confusing the new product α²β² with (α+β)·(αβ) — it is (αβ)², not (αβ)(α+β)

nodes.title: Verify the cubic zero–coefficient relations numerically
nodes.brief: Compute the sum, pair-sum, and product of the three zeroes and match them to −b/a, c/a, −d/a.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Add the three zeroes to verify the sum relation.
For ax³+bx²+cx+d with zeroes α, β, γ, compute α+β+γ and check it equals −b/a. For 3x³−5x²−11x−3 (a=3, b=−5) with zeroes 3, −1, −1/3: sum = 3+(−1)+(−1/3) = 2−1/3 = 5/3 = −(−5)/3 ✓.

PART 2 — Compute all three pairwise products to verify the pair-sum.
Form αβ+βγ+γα and check it equals c/a (no minus sign). αβ=3×(−1)=−3; βγ=(−1)×(−1/3)=1/3; γα=(−1/3)×3=−1. Sum = −3+1/3−1 = −4+1/3 = −12/3+1/3 = −11/3 = (−11)/3 ✓.

PART 3 — Multiply all three zeroes to verify the product relation.
Compute αβγ and check it equals −d/a (has minus sign). αβγ = 3×(−1)×(−1/3) = 1 = −(−3)/3 ✓. Memory aid: sum and product carry the minus (−b/a and −d/a); pair-sum does not (c/a).
keyMoves: Add the three zeroes for the sum
Match each to the coefficient ratio
misconceptions: Forgetting one of the three pairwise products
Sign errors with fractional zeroes

refreshers.trigger: adding fractions over a common denominator and multiplying fractions (including a whole number with a fraction)
refreshers.surfacingQuestion: quick one before we add these zeroes: what is 2 - 1/3, and what is 3 * (-1/3)?
ladder: A fraction like 1/3 is one part out of three equal parts; a whole number can be written as a fraction too, e.g. 2 = 6/3.
To add or subtract fractions you first make the bottoms (denominators) the same, then add or subtract just the tops: 2 - 1/3 = 6/3 - 1/3 = 5/3.
To multiply fractions you multiply the tops together and the bottoms together: 3 * (1/3) = (3*1)/(1*3) = 3/3 = 1.
Watch the signs while you do it: 3 * (-1/3) = -3/3 = -1, and (-1) * (-1/3) = +1/3.
refreshers.answer: To add or subtract fractions, give them a common denominator and then combine the tops: 2 - 1/3 = 6/3 - 1/3 = 5/3, and -3 + 1/3 - 1 = -12/3 + 1/3 = -11/3. To multiply fractions, multiply tops by tops and bottoms by bottoms, keeping track of signs: 3*(-1/3) = -3/3 = -1 and 3*(-1)*(-1/3) = 1. That is exactly the arithmetic in this node - the three zeroes 3, -1, -1/3 are added to get the sum 5/3, their pairwise products are combined over a common denominator to get -11/3, and all three are multiplied to get 1.
refreshers.returnCue: with fraction adding and multiplying in hand, let's run the three checks: sum, pair-sum, then product.

narrative:
## What I read (review pass)

I re-read the full segmented transcription of NCERT Class 10 Maths chapter jemh102 "Polynomials" (`extractions/_text/jemh102.txt`, 6 SEGs: 2.1 Introduction, 2.2 Geometrical Meaning of Zeroes, EXERCISE 2.1, 2.3 Relationship between Zeroes and Coefficients, EXERCISE 2.2, 2.4 Summary) and the shared CBSE Maths SQP + Marking Scheme (`_maths-exams.txt`, Standard paper Q1 and Q8 with answer keys).

Scope note: this transcription does NOT contain a Division Algorithm section — the chapter ends at 2.3/Summary even though SEG 0's intro line mentions it. Recorded in absentNotes; no nodes invented for it.

## Corrections applied to the draft

1. GOAL CITATION (D-120 fix). The draft cited the goal node `relate-zeroes-and-coefficients-of-polynomials` at sec=3 with the quote "In this chapter, we will try to answer these questions. We will also study the division algorithm for polynomials." That line lives in SEG 0 (not SEG 3) AND it foregrounds the division algorithm, which the chapter never delivers. Re-quoted to the SEG-0 line that actually states the chapter's goal — "are the zeroes of a quadratic polynomial also related to its coefficients?" — and fixed sec to 0. This is a verbatim substring of SEG 0.

2. EDGE FIX — second root removed. `bound-number-of-zeroes-by-degree` appeared only as a parent (of `count-zeroes-from-graph` and `identify-degree-of-polynomial`) and never as a child, so the graph had TWO roots. Added a true prerequisite edge `relate-zeroes-and-coefficients-of-polynomials → bound-number-of-zeroes-by-degree` (knowing how many zeroes a polynomial can have is a genuine prerequisite to relating those zeroes to coefficients). Now a single root with no orphans.

3. GATE srcLabel cleanup. Two CAS/numeric gates carried the placeholder srcLabel "2.3 (worked example p.x)" — the page-range marker was an artifact. Renamed to "2.3 (worked example 2x² − 8x + 6)" so the locator is meaningful; the verbatim quote is unchanged and remains the authoritative citation.

## Verification performed

- CITATIONS: every node quote re-checked as a verbatim substring of its SEG. All 21 verify (including the multi-line cubic-relations quote at lines 236-238 and the signed-substitution line at 21).
- GATES: 21 gates, one per node, 100% coverage. Every gate quote re-checked verbatim — chapter gates against jemh102 SEGs, the two exam gates against `_maths-exams.txt` Marking Scheme lines "1. D) -6,6" and "8. B) 8𝑥2- 20" (copied with the OCR's literal mathematical-bold "𝑥"). Each grader/expected/answer is self-consistent for the deterministic tester (numeric values match, mcq `correct` is among `options`, CAS forms are single-variable in x).
- EDGES: acyclic (all edges flow from higher skill toward bedrock; no back-edges), single root, no orphan node, every endpoint is a defined slug.
- GRAIN: each node is one failable step. Example 5's substitution check (`verify-cubic-zero-by-substitution`) is kept separate from the relations check (`verify-cubic-zero-coefficient-relations`), per one-failable-thing-one-node. No pure-exposition node (history of the word "quadratic", the parabola-shape cases, the no-zero case) was promoted to a skill.

## What was absent / uncertain

- Division Algorithm, dividing-out a known factor, hand-plotting graphs (explicitly excluded by the chapter footnote), and the formal proof of the cubic relations are all genuinely absent — recorded in absentNotes, not failed.
- Goal gate Q8: the OCR garbles the zeroes ("− √5 / 2 and √5 / 2"); the keyed answer 8x²−20 is taken as canonical from the Marking Scheme regardless of the arithmetic ambiguity, since the gate is a deterministic MCQ against the published key.
absentNotes: This chapter transcription teaches ONLY: classifying polynomials by degree, evaluating polynomials / defining zeroes, the geometric meaning of zeroes (reading and counting them from graphs), and the zero–coefficient relationships for quadratics and cubics (both verifying them and constructing a polynomial from a given sum and product). A fuller treatment would also cover, but THIS transcription does NOT teach (so these are omitted, not failed): (1) the Division Algorithm for Polynomials — polynomial long division and p(x)=g(x)×q(x)+r(x) — despite SEG 0's intro line promising it, the section is absent here; (2) finding all zeroes of a cubic/quartic given two known zeroes by dividing out the known quadratic factor; (3) plotting/sketching parabolas and cubics by hand (explicitly excluded by the chapter footnote: "Plotting of graphs of quadratic or cubic polynomials is not meant to be done by the students, nor is to be evaluated"); (4) the formal proof/derivation of the cubic relations (stated as "it can be proved" without proof); (5) forming a new polynomial from transformed zeroes such as α², β² (this appears in CBSE SQP problems but the chapter text itself never demonstrates it, so no node — only its prerequisite sum/product skills are taught here).
